stem: Using the substitution \( u = x^{3} + [[c]] \) and changing the limits of integration accordingly, evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[a]]} 3x^{2}\left(x^{3} + [[c]]\right)^{[[n]]}\,dx \).
solution: With \( u = x^{3} + [[c]] \) we have \( du = 3x^{2}\,dx \), and the limits change from \( x = 0,\,[[a]] \) to \( u = [[c]],\,[[a]]^{3} + [[c]] \). Then \( \int u^{[[n]]}\,du = \left.\tfrac{u^{[[n]]+1}}{[[n]]+1}\right| = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses only the upper limit and forgets to subtract the value at the lower limit.
This divides by 3 even though \( 3x^{2}\,dx \) already equals \( du \) exactly.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by three from the correct value.

stem: Using the LINEAR substitution \( u = [[m]]x + [[k]] \) (so \( du = [[m]]\,dx \)) and changing the limits from \( x=0,\,[[a]] \) to \( u=[[lo_u]],\,[[hi_u]] \), evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[a]]} [[c]]\left([[m]]x + [[k]]\right)^{[[n]]}\,dx \).
solution: With \( u=[[m]]x+[[k]] \), \( dx = \dfrac{du}{[[m]]} \), and the limits become \( u=[[lo_u]] \) to \( u=[[hi_u]] \). Then \( [[c]]\displaystyle\int_{[[lo_u]]}^{[[hi_u]]} \dfrac{u^{[[n]]}}{[[m]]}\,du = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[m]]}\cdot\dfrac{u^{[[n]]+1}}{[[n]]+1}\bigg|_{[[lo_u]]}^{[[hi_u]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by \( [[m]] \) to convert \( dx \) into \( du \).
This multiplies by \( [[m]] \) instead of dividing by it, the opposite mistake.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: Using the substitution \( u = \sin x \) (so \( du = \cos x\,dx \)) and changing the limits from \( x=0,\,\pi/2 \) to \( u=0,\,1 \), evaluate \( \int_{0}^{\pi/2} [[c]]\sin^{[[n]]} x\cos x\,dx \).
solution: With \( u=\sin x \), the integral becomes \( [[c]]\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1} u^{[[n]]}\,du = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[n]]+1}u^{[[n]]+1}\bigg|_{0}^{1} = [[answer]] \); no trigonometric evaluation of the antiderivative is needed once the limits are changed to \( u \)-values.
distractor_rationales: This uses \( n \) instead of \( n+1 \) in the denominator of the power rule.
This multiplies instead of dividing by \( n+1 \), inverting the power rule.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: Using the substitution \( u = e^{x} + [[a]] \) (so \( du = e^{x}\,dx \)) and changing the limits from \( x = 0,\,\ln [[b]] \) to \( u = 1+[[a]],\,[[b]]+[[a]] \), evaluate \( \int_{0}^{\ln [[b]]} [[c]]e^{x}\left(e^{x} + [[a]]\right)^{[[n]]}\,dx \).
solution: With \( u=e^{x}+[[a]] \), the exponential's derivative supplies \( du \) exactly. The limits become \( u=1+[[a]] \) (at \( x=0 \)) and \( u=[[b]]+[[a]] \) (at \( x=\ln [[b]] \)), so \( [[c]]\displaystyle\int_{1+[[a]]}^{[[b]]+[[a]]} u^{[[n]]}\,du = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[n]]+1}\left(([[b]]+[[a]])^{[[n]]+1} - (1+[[a]])^{[[n]]+1}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to shift the limits by \( [[a]] \), using \( u=1 \) and \( u=[[b]] \) instead of \( u=1+[[a]] \) and \( u=[[b]]+[[a]] \).
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: Using the substitution \( u = \ln x \) (so \( du = \dfrac{dx}{x} \)) and changing the limits from \( x=1,\,e^{[[k]]} \) to \( u=0,\,[[k]] \), evaluate \( \int_{1}^{e^{[[k]]}} \frac{[[c]]\left(\ln x\right)^{[[n]]}}{x}\,dx \).
solution: With \( u=\ln x \), the factor \( \tfrac{1}{x}\,dx \) is exactly \( du \); the limits become \( u=\ln 1 = 0 \) and \( u=\ln(e^{[[k]]})=[[k]] \). So \( [[c]]\displaystyle\int_{0}^{[[k]]} u^{[[n]]}\,du = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[n]]+1}[[k]]^{[[n]]+1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by \( n+1 \) after applying the power rule.
This uses \( k^{n+1} \) but forgets the \( 1/(n+1) \) scaling, an off-by-power error.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: Using the substitution \( u = \sqrt{x} \) (so \( du = \dfrac{dx}{2\sqrt{x}} \)) and changing the limits from \( x=0,\,[[asq]] \) to \( u=0,\,[[a]] \), evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[asq]]} \frac{[[c]]\left(1+\sqrt{x}\right)^{[[n]]}}{\sqrt{x}}\,dx \).
solution: With \( u=\sqrt{x} \), \( \dfrac{dx}{\sqrt{x}} = 2\,du \), so the integral becomes \( 2[[c]]\displaystyle\int_{0}^{[[a]]}(1+u)^{[[n]]}\,du = \dfrac{2[[c]]}{[[n]]+1}\left((1+[[a]])^{[[n]]+1} - 1\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of 2 that comes from \( dx/\sqrt{x}=2\,du \).
This uses the value at the upper limit alone without integrating (a plug-in error), and drops the 1/(n+1) scaling.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.
